speaker: interviewer
text: Henry Wermuth, and this is reel number 10.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, now, we had just discussed--

speaker: interviewer
text: The new style--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Underwear.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my father's practical discovery because the paper of the cement bags, which we now wear, wore as underwear, did not let any cold through, or wind, cold wind through. And it really worked. Now, for a while it went well well. Then we made a mistake. We saw somebody we felt sorry for and told him about it. And soon, more and more people knew about it and knew-- more and more people used it. And one evening on the way back, we were stopped at the actual, at the certain gate going into the camps where we slept, there's a-- the part where we slept. And we were sort of searched. And kapos, the sadists, discovered these shirts, and people were beaten very, very badly. I think there were even a few killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you beaten as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they had found enough to cool the, the-- you know, the energy must have run out because by the time we came-- we were the last again-- it were sort of, you know, quite a, you know, they stopped quite a bit before us. But we did not dare any more. The result was I got a very sore throat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was the second and only time I was ill in the camps, except of course, the other sort of weight loss-- oh yeah, the diarrhea not counting. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I found out there's something else incredible. I was put into a hospital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] a fellow approached me, a so-called nurse. And I was wondering-- he was actually limping. Well, persons with any handicap usually don't survive. Anyway, maybe as a nurse, because of that, he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he spoke to me, and we found out he was also from the same town although we haven't met before-- from Frankfurt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he gave me, again, do you want some more food? Yes. And he brought me, again, the diet soup.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it tasted nice, and on top of what else we had, all right. Not, not, not having worked, it was all right. But I was, I preferred if he could give me any other food. Perhaps he did. I didn't, I didn't sort of remember that much because I was more interested in my mind, my mind was more interested with other incidents. Next to me was somebody else who had open tuberculosis. And he must have given me the germ there and then. But it only manifested itself when I was x-rayed for going to America in 1947. And that stopped me from, that stopped me from going to America. Otherwise, my fate would have taken a different turn as well. I wouldn't have married you either. So-- probably wouldn't have met you then. That's what I meant. Anyway, but this person next to me had tuberculosis, but I got better. But no work. Lying in bed was better than going outside. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But then he said, can I see you tonight? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I thought, yes, why not? With this man from Frankfurt, let's discuss where our ways may have, may have met before. Was visiting me in the same night and was sitting next to me first. And then he was lying down, and I moved over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what I found strange that he also put a blanket, my blanket, over him. I assumed, well, while he was listening to my story and feeling sort of like duly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, show, showing that he was feeling for my losses et cetera-- probably had his own. But he was interested more in my hand. I moved my hand nearer to his part of the body which I didn't want to touch and was near enough. In the middle of talking, without interrupting the talk, I took it back. And I pretended that nothing happened because you can, cannot afford to make any enemies or bad friends in such situations. But he soon cut the talk short, went away, I didn't see him again except once. After three weeks, he said, you better leave because an SS doctor is coming. And not many people leave the hospital. Most go right away where there is a cure forever. You know what it means. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I left. And ever there was a truth in the saying, from the fire into the, from the frying pan into the fire, that was it. Hardly had I entered my own barracks, I was told to undress because the SS doctor just inspected the people there. Already, there was a line already in front, going to another room which I had never entered because it was probably the private rooms of the block eldest, usually like a couple and mostly like a German criminal. And I haven't, I haven't told you about it yet. Of course, you must have heard it 100 times when you interview people, they had a green triangle. There were two different kind of green triangles. One point up and one point down. I'm not sure which way-- one of them was the political type of outcast from Germany. The other one were criminals. And we had a red and a yellow triangle on top of each other with a number. And say we were Jews. There were others who had red ones. There were also political prisoners. And there were purple ones and black ones and, you know, all sorts of homosexuals, and those who were there for religious reasons, outcasts, I'm not sure, priests-- a number of triangles. But I didn't make a study of it then, and so I cannot be asked details now. Anyway, these were probably the rooms of the block elders. We went through a small door into that. And we were undressed. But we already had filled up nicely. And my father said, put out your tummy to look even better than you are. And my father also had to wait, and he was behind me. And I stepped through the door, and I saw the SS doctor. And next to him, the block eldest as he was called, block eldester. And the SS man's thumb went accordingly to the right or to the left. And to the right were the people were the healthy-looking people. And to the left were those who one could see they were, well, he selected for the gas chambers to be cured. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in front of me was a fellow who looked much better than the one in the front of him who passed. But for some reason, the, the SS man turned, and his thumb went to the left. And the helpers who who stood by pushed him into the other. For them there's-- they couldn't care anymore-- into the other little part of the room with the condemned. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had, at that moment, no other thought, and don't look him in the eye. And look straight out and, as though I didn't care. And of course, put my tummy out. No problem today, mind you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And while I passed easily and then when I reached the other side, I stood still for a moment. Where is my father? Why is he not right behind me? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't dare to turn around. It went through my mind, what's going to happen if he was turned to the other side? Am I going to wave him bye-bye, or am I going to join him? And then he did come. And then I said something which is typical of me but not the nice me. In such moments, I say something usually-- very often say something youthful, bravura or something unsuitable? I said, actually, what kept you? It was meant to be a joke, but glad like the warm water in the shower, glad to-- it was over. He was here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then actually I was told that there was a hesitation for a minute and the words were, said, this time, because not that he looked bad but his age. So, anyway, I was worried about the word this time because that meant maybe next time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We survived that. And out of 600 barracks, only 28 were taken. And we discussed that in the evening. It must have been either a kind doctor. He looked like, I saw later, a picture of Mengele. Could have been him, but we thought it was somebody else. He was too kind. Only so few. And, on the other hand, my father said, maybe not. Maybe the whole-- they're, they're mellowed because someone said the gassing had stopped. And you know, the ghost of winter was going to-- winter, 19-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 44- 45. And what, you know, is that it was clear that Germany was going to lose the war. And they were already preparing in their mind for escape. And why should they load themselves more than they already had on their, on their, sort of, conscience-- if they had a conscience. Whether the gas chambers were working with the new transport, I don't know. But these people who were selected I'm sure went into the gas chambers. Anyway, this was our conversation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Unless I can think of something else during that time-- yeah, perhaps. Oh, no, I'll make it short. There was once an incident that I, the men, the German civilians, stood behind me and made fun of my drawings. He said, you're not the German sort of pun, where you said you are not a, a, a-- maler is, to mal is paint and also grinding with a miller, you know, those, you know, grinding or something, you know. And he said you're not a maler, a painter, but you are a maler, sort of meaning the others of the mill. And of course I was afraid. But I proved and he was quite happy with me in the end. That was only the other incident. Oh, yes. One more, perhaps, in Auschwitz. Where others were very, very cold in the last days in Auschwitz and working in the streets and had-- and hungry and dying, I was in a very small room, away from my father who also had an easy job, painting some letters on some electrical sort of boxes. Something I could have done in two days, I worked on for three or four weeks. And, and I had enough to eat with it. And that time, in my-- I described it in more details in my book. I had time to think, and I was thinking of what would I do with after the war and how would I hug and kiss and never quarrel again if I would meet my mother and sister again. Or take a machine gun to kill Germans. Kill, kill, kill Germans. And I was actually shooting in my mind, in my semi-dreams, the Germans. And suddenly I saw faces I knew when I stopped. And I also saw children. I thought, well, children have been killed by the Germans as well. And I got-- carried on shooting. But even in my sort of dream or semi-dream, I aimed higher. The awakening, of course, was that I was like every awakening, you're back where you were. Shortly after that, one day, the Russians had made another offensive, and we were evacuated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After another march-- not death march. The death march came at the very end, but it really probably had started then. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We came to another either nearby camp or station, whatever it was. And it's-- the memories go vague sometimes in timing and small details. There was a train we had to get onto. And I was looking out, and I could see Max Spira or Adolf Konig again. After all, these were people of, you know, probably prepare themselves with more food and everything. I didn't. I just wanted-- my father had just stepped onto one of the wagons, and I saw a number of kapos going into a different-- to the front of the train. And some of them carrying, carrying parcels, some of them carrying mattress, mattresses. And I, with a unfilial jerk, I pulled my father from the train again. And we went after them. It was going into the lion's den, but we were lucky before. So we found ourselves in the middle of a wagon with kapos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. Never. Probably, they didn't either. But they made themselves, you know, mattresses and food. I'm sure they had enough. It was the first wagon in a long train. And as we went, as we stepped into the wagon, helping out to lay out the mattresses, one of the, some of the kapos shushed away other prisoners who wanted to get on. Away!

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the doors closed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And a voice said to me, what are you doing here? Out!

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, no. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You can throw me out but not alive. And I stood. And I knew my father would stand behind me. And he looked around, and somebody sort of waved him off. And we were, the two of us, amongst other 36 kapos, most of them, I'm sure, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] besties, beasts. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But, actually, they had no more-- at that time they were prisoners. Their function had ended. But soon, although I had-- we were-- the wagon was full of blankets, of mattresses-- we're lying, sleeping, eating, everything on blankets-- but we're only 38. And we could stretch out. And really lie down. And at night we slept under a layer of blankets. The only thing, regret I had, is theirs was an open wagon. And it was January. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the other wagons, at least, were closed. Little did I know that I had another guardian angel guiding me there. But that comes a bit later. Once I woke up, early morning, I looked at my left. And my father was still asleep. I looked out, and it was like a giant shroud with people and valleys in between, valleys in between. It was an eerie view, like a cemetery, all corpses, nobody moved. Well, eventually, we got up. And throughout the snow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] little did I know that people next door were licking this bit of snow, and it came into the crevices of the wider windows because they were dying of thirst and hunger. And when, where they started a train, a wagon full of people, sooner or later, as I described before, somebody got tired. The other sat on top of him, and it must have been terrible. And we were, according to that, we were going in luxury. Not only this, we actually had warm water. And for the first time, we gained great respect because we were the-- with our big menashkas, containing one litre and three litres, the man from the engine could pass us hot water. And we were given enough food, you know, for that, for the kapos and everything. We were in comparative luxury. But we were three weeks going probably for one camp to another, and nobody could take us. At one stage, practically between, well, middle of the journey, I saw one [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sign on front, on top of me-- Prague, Prague. I remember the thoughts. No, today, nowadays, traveling is nothing. In those days, traveling was only for the few and the rich. One day, I will tell my children that I also have been in Prague. Anyway, we were only there for an hour or two, and then the train went on. And then the train stopped after three weeks in front of the camp which we later learned was Mauthausen. It also had names like Dora or Mittelbau, where the famous V-1 and V-2 weapons were manufactured. And we thought, oh, something important again. Little did we know that this was a camp where prisoners have been wasted like nothing on earth. 30,000 to 40,000 people died just because of the way they were worked. I was there for eight days, you know, which, not much to tell. Yes, another hanging took place because people were captured. Some people had escaped and were captured-- three people this time. But instead of front of standing in front of about 10,000, I stood at the very rear of about 20,000, 30,000, I'm not sure. And I only saw it from the very great distance. And I didn't see even the faces, but I saw three people hanging. That's the only incident because there were three captured after, you know, being, sort of after escaping. And obviously, it also, it weighs on your mind if you see these things because it hampers your plans for escape. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, after eight days, we were taken away to another camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train again, but a very short journey away. And during this journey, some young fellow whom I had seen in Auschwitz talked to me. But I was listening half, but the other half was listening for possibly crevices or loose boards, possibly to escape. And he told me again stories from Auschwitz like a certain person in Auschwitz-- I only remember one name, and I remembered it later again. I came across it. The name was Boga. And he [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had a contraption in his office, the so-called Boga swing, where he stripped prisoners, put them-- hung them up like an airplane, and with sticks-- maybe on the body and often on the genitals-- hit them to make them fly. And the invitation given out for the nearby officers and ladies, so-called ladies, SS women to come in and look at the performance. Anyway, one such person spoke to him. They were sad. They said I am finished. I won't survive it. Remember me. Remember my name, and he told me the name, but I haven't remembered it. I remembered Boga, the name of Boga of the SS men, and the men later. I think in, in Frankfurt, there was the not the Nuremberg Process. There was another one in Frankfurt-- Auschwitz Process. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this Boga was then, I sort of, I think, a pretty good life. Of course, he wasn't guilty. Somebody else did all of the killing and what ever he did. But that's-- I wanted to sort of switch from that. And while he was still talking, the train stopped. And we went out to a place which in my English version I called the [? Hard ?] Mountains, the camp, and somewhere in the [? Hard ?] Mountains because I forgot the name. On a couple or three years ago or so in Germany, somebody said, I'm going on holiday to Osterode. And I had a, that was the name where we were. Anyway, I remember we were working three weeks in a tunnel. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my, our work was probably to dig for, against, you know, for the hierarchy of the, of the, of the regime, you know. Maybe they were already put berths for maybe atom bombs in it because they were working themselves on, on these things actually that didn't make much progress because the one person who could have helped them to create the Wunderwaffen, the military weapons which Hitler promised and never managed to finish it was Einstein. But as you know, he had to emigrate. And he helped the, the Americans. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Can't be-- could not be thought out if, what Hitler would have done if he had the atom bomb. Anyway, the, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there, the work was very hard. Again, I asked the German worker, engineers who was drilling, if he could help him. He says, yes, you can. And for the next couple of weeks or so, I thought I had a very easy job. I was sitting back-to-back with him. He had the main brunt of the drill, the kango-type drill, whatever it was, primitive, I'm sure, to, against what, how they drill today.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my bones were shaking so much that in the beginning, I was really afraid that I would collapse. And, but then I said, how to get it secondhand? But from time to time, I would, so he gave me a bit of bread, which, of course, I shared with my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the others were very envious. And I, at one time, thought, well, you can change with me some because it was-- I felt terrible at the beginning. But I got used to it at the end. And one day after three weeks, weeks without anything at all, marching to work, we're taken away, again in a train. And unfortunately, without our menashkas. Now we had nothing. And we ended up in a place called Helmstedt, a very small place where they have never seen Jews. Mostly they were prisoners of war, German criminals, and a mixture of things, about, perhaps, four, five, six, seven all together, including us, 150 of us. They had no work. But I soon found work-- artificial work. It must have been on a hill because we went downward, downhill for 20 minutes. And it was light and easy and wonderful in the morning. But in the evening, to go uphill after being beaten terribly, hard work, and little food. Anyway, our group shrunk and shrunk very quickly. And again, we did something which only we could do because we had two of us sticking together. I found there was a nearby hut. And inside the hut were a lot of boxes on top of each other. I climbed on top of the boxes and down the other side. And one of the-- I put one of the boxes on top of me and there I could rest. Now, probably, well of course, we exchanged with our-- we changed over from time to time. If my father would either whistle or throw a stone, it would be the sign either to go home or to change or danger or anything. Now, this helped us a great deal. And I could swear that the other one was helped by knowing the hard worker was helped knowing the other one had to rest. Yeah.
